topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the canvas, a thick, obsidian line snaking across the vibrant depiction of a stormy sea. It wasn't part of the original composition. It was a stark, almost brutal geometric pattern, executed in a style so different from her own fluid brushstrokes. Her hand, usually so steady, trembled slightly as she traced the inked lines with a fingertip. Who had done this? The texture of the ink felt alien against the oil paint, a foreign intrusion into her carefully constructed vision. She leaned back, rubbing her temples, the silence of the studio suddenly amplifying the sheer strangeness of the situation. "Impossible," she murmured to the empty room, her voice tight.

[story 2]

I found it tucked beneath the preliminary sketches—a tiny, almost hidden piece of work. It was a delicate, hyper-realistic portrait of my own hand, rendered in a style I’d never attempted. The shading was masterful, the detail unnerving. I picked up a magnifying glass, holding it close to the pigment. My breath hitched. This wasn't my hand. It was too perfect, too deliberate. A cold knot tightened in my stomach as I realized the implication: someone had taken a piece of my creation and overlaid it with their own mark. I dropped the glass onto the desk with a soft clatter, the sound echoing the sudden, unsettling emptiness in the air.

[story 3]

Marcus circled the sculpture slowly, his gaze sweeping over the bronze form. Near the base, where the surface was meant to be smooth patina, there was a deliberate etching. It was a series of swirling, almost tribal markings, etched with a precision that belied the rough texture of the metal. He stopped, his posture stiffening. He ran a thumb over the mark, feeling the slight indentation. His brow furrowed deeply, his mouth set in a thin, unreadable line. He took a step back, his eyes darting around the gallery as if expecting an explanation to materialize from the shadows. "What is this?" he asked, his voice low, edged with a sharp uncertainty.
